data.title: Upwork: Profits Surge, Upside Remains
data.description:
Introduction

Upwork (NASDAQ:UPWK) posted a great Q1'25 on the 5th of May, posting significant margin improvements and raising adjusted EBITDA guidance, with shares up around 17%. In early February 2025, I wrote a bullish article on Upwork highlighting the platform’s untapped operational leverage, strong network effects, and what I expected to be favorable labor market shifts. I argued that concerns that AI would crowd out jobs on Upwork were largely overstated and that the company had the potential to scale profitably like other platform businesses (e.g. Uber, Airbnb) as well as benefitting from a more flexible, project-based hiring environment.

While Upwork's Q1'25 was great, it has only modestly outperformed the S&P since my last piece on the company. The reminder of this article will review Upwork's most recent quarter along with my updated outlook, to then consider valuation and conclude.

Review of Q1'25

Revenue came in at $192.7 million in Q1, up 1% year-over-year. Gross Services Volume (GSV) was $987.7 million (down 2% YoY), but GSV per active client rose 3% as existing clients spend more on average. Net income was $37.7 million, more than double the $18.4 million a year ago. Adjusted EBITDA came in at $56.0 million, up 68% YoY, for a record 29% adjusted EBITDA margin (versus 17% in Q1 2024) . In short, margins expanded dramatically even on minimal revenue growth, as R&D, sales and marketing, and G&A, were all lower as a percentage of revenues in Q1'25.

AI, what many feared would make a lot of work on Upwork redundant, instead looks to have boosted the business both in terms of AI talent hired through the website, and through Upwork's own AI assistant Uma. In Q1, AI-related freelance spend on Upwork rose 25% YoY. Moreover, instead of taking away jobs from Upwork, management emphasized that the categories of work perceived to be most threatened by AI were in fact flat or grew. Upwork's own work on AI, called Uma, also looks to be performing well, which is a huge value-add that can justify higher take-rates.

On the earnings call, management also committed to entirely offset stock-based compensation with share buybacks which has been rather significant at around 10% of revenues in the last quarters.

Zooming out a bit, a concern is that Upwork's growth appears to have stalled, with GSV shrinking for the first time in 2024.

I still believe that the core tailwinds of Upwork that I identified in my first article are in place: enhanced networking effects, a labor market with more churn and project-based hiring, together with a model that allows significant operational leverage. This should bode well for Upwork into the future, especially as AI concern are (at least for now) alleviated.

Valuation

Even with shares up 17% after posting their first quarter, it doesn't look like the market has extraordinary expectations for the future. The models below assumes analyst's mean revenue estimates until 2027 and growth assumptions thereafter.

3% growth appears very conservative and a 11% FCF margin as well given the potential for operating leverage inherent in Upwork's capital light model. In Q1'25, they posted an FCF margin of 16%. I think my case is not too optimistic, and I would say at the current price shares are undervalued, presenting 56% upside.

The main risks to my thesis is how much Upwork can squeeze their take rate before competition becomes a concern. If the labor market weakens significantly, that may be trouble, although Upwork is an interesting case as they may benefit from labor market disruptions.

Conclusion

Upwork posted a stellar Q1'25 with the potential of their model shining through, with all operating expenses down and a 20% operating margin. Operating expenses as a % of revenue is still significantly lower at companies with similar network models such as Uber (at 25%) and AirBnB (at around 50%), suggesting Upwork still has room to further improve operational leverage. Given what I think are conservative assumptions, I see the shares having around 50% upside from current levels.

data.title: Dell: A Mixed Bag But Still A Buy (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Intro

After I initiated Dell Technologies Inc. (NYSE:DELL) at a buy rating in early January, the stock has fallen over 20%. I believe the stock is a mixed bag currently but remains a buy overall. In the below analysis, despite a continued downtrend, the majority of technicals show a mixed outlook. For the fundamentals, Q4 earnings were also mixed while guidance shows that revenue growth is expected to slow while EPS growth accelerates. While the fundamentals are a bit shaky, the P/S ratio's around average level relative to the past few years seems to undervalue stock. The major valuation discount relative to the IT sector also looks excessive. Therefore, I reiterate Dell at a buy rating on an attractive valuation even as the technicals and financials of the company are mixed currently.

Technicals

Charting

Trends

Since late November, Dell has been in a downtrend and a channel seems to have developed since then. Despite the stock's recent recovery, it remains in this channel and so further losses may occur. Furthermore, a near term uptrend that started at the early April low has ended as the stock's stalling momentum has broken the trend line.

Support

In addition to the lower channel line, there are three other support levels of which the nearest is at around 89. This area was previously support twice back in March. The next support level is at about 84. This was a recent upside gap up in late April that may have contributed to some support on the last trading day of April. Lastly, there is support in the high 60s as an early April bullish engulfing pattern occurred there.

Resistance

Other than the upper channel line and the former near term uptrend line, there are also three nearby resistance levels. The first is at the key round number 100. This level was support in late January while being resistance in March. Moving up, the area at about 108 could also be resistance as that level represents a late February gap down on heavy volume. Lastly, the 120 level may be resistance as it acted as resistance on multiple occasions from December to February.

Takeaway

The chart is overall quite bearish in my view. Despite there being a reasonable amount of support below Dell, the stock's uptrend has ended and the longer term downward channel remains in effect. Resistance above the stock is also considerable and so the odds likely favour further downside despite the appearance of bullish engulfing pattern a while ago.

Moving Averages

Simple Moving Average [SMA]

The 50 day SMA and the 200 day SMA had a death cross back in January as a long term signal of weakness. The gap between these lines continues to expand and so bearish momentum is continuing to build. The 200 day SMA last acted as resistance in February while the 50 day SMA may have pressured the stock as of late.

Exponential Moving Average [EMA]

The 9 day EMA and 21 day EMA had a bullish crossover in late April to show that the near term was positive. The deviation between these two lines still remains decent to show that near term upward momentum is still present. However, with the 9 day EMA's trajectory levelling out, this bullish state may not last for long.

Bollinger Bands

Dell stock broke above the Bollinger Bands midline in a late April gap up. This was a sign that the downtrend was weakening and also a confirmation signal for the new uptrend in the stock. Dell is currently still above the midline but if it were to dip below, it would provide reassurance that the downtrend remains strong. With the midline's trajectory turning sharply upwards, that may soon occur.

Takeaway

Overall, I believe there are some mixed signals in these moving averages. The SMAs clearly show that the long term remains bearish for Dell while the EMAs reflect that the near term strength is still holding on by a small margin. For the Bollinger bands, the stock remains above the midline but that may change if the stock doesn't stage a rally in the near future.

Indicators

Moving Average Convergence Divergence [MACD]

The MACD had a bullish crossover with its signal line back in early to mid April. After the gap between these lines expanded to show strong upward momentum, the histogram bars have been bearish since the end of April. This shows that the stock's recovery is running out of steam. The MACD also made a six month low in early April and so it did not confirm the bullish engulfing pattern.

Relative Strength Index [RSI]

The RSI currently has a reading of 54.86 which shows that the bulls remain in modest control of the stock currently. It has recovered from below 30 in early April and so there has been quite a large increase in strength. Speaking of that early April reading of below 30, it was also a six month low, another indication that the bullish engulfing pattern should not have been trusted.

Stochastics

After a bearish crossover at the end of April, the %K just crossed back above the %D for a bullish crossover in recent days. This is at odds with the other indications that the rally in the stock is weakening but investors should still monitor this indicator for a potential of a false signal. Both lines also remain above 80 and so the bulls are clearly still dominant currently.

Takeaway

In my view, the mixed signals continue into the indicators. While the MACD shows that upward momentum is stalling, the RSI shows that the bulls remain in control and the stochastics just had a bullish crossover. Therefore, further near term strength should still not be ruled out. That said, the MACD and RSI didn't confirm the early April bullish engulfing pattern and so fresh six month lows are also a possibility.

Fibonacci Retracement

Using the late November high and the early April low, Fibonacci Retracement targets are shown above. During Dell's recent rebound, it first saw some resistance at the 23.6% retracement but it was ultimately able to gap up above that level. While the stock never touched the 38.2% retracement, it still seems that it may have been a bit of resistance. With the uncertainty as shown in the moving averages and indicators, there is some potential that the stock may be a bit range bound between the 23.6% and 38.2% retracement levels until there is a clearer shift in the balance of power between the bulls and bears. However, with the stock seemingly stalling far before getting to the 50% retracement, odds likely favour a continuation of the downtrend from here.

Fundamentals

Earnings

Quarterly Results

Dell reported their FY2025 Q4 earnings in late February and showed mixed results overall. Firstly, the company was able to generate revenues of $23.9 billion, representing a YoY increase of 7%. This missed expectations by $638.66 million, potentially showing shaky business activity. Furthermore, adjusted gross margin declined slightly from 24.8% to 24.3% and so there may be pricing pressure. As shown above, the current revenue growth rate still remains far above the multiyear average, however. In addition, their highly important Infrastructure Solutions Group segment reported revenue growth of 22% YoY to $11.4 billion as AI tailwinds continue. For EPS, Dell reported an adjusted figure of $2.68, up 18% from the prior year period. This figure was able to beat expectations by a healthy amount of $0.16. Lastly, for operating cash flows they reported only $585 million. This could be a potentially worrying sign with Q4's figure being the lowest amount since FY2023 Q3 as shown in the chart above.

Guidance

Dell provided guidance for their FY2026 Q1 in their earnings report. For revenue, they are expecting a range of between $22.5 billion and $23.5 billion. At the midpoint, YoY growth would therefore be 3%. This is a notable slowdown in revenue growth from Q4's 7%. However, revenue growth is still expected to remain above the average of the past few years. For EPS, Dell is expecting an adjusted figure of $1.65, representing growth of 25% over the year ago quarter. This would be an acceleration in growth for this figure as Q4's growth rate was only 18%. Overall, I believe guidance was mixed as the slowdown in revenue growth suggests that business activity will weaken while accelerating EPS growth shows that profitability and efficiency is set to strengthen. As of this writing, Seeking Alpha has a Revisions Grade of C for Dell, also showing that the outlook is mixed for the company.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio [P/S]

The P/S ratio has seen major contraction since the early to mid 2024 peak. Since the recent 2025 low, the multiple has recovered somewhat to a current reading of 0.704. I believe the stock is undervalued according to this measure. As discussed above, revenue growth remains above average for their Q4 despite being a bit sluggish at only 7%. In addition, in their Q1 guidance they stated that growth will slow to only 3%. However, that figure would still remain above the multiyear average. While the fundamentals are a bit shaky, the P/S ratio's contraction still seems overdone as the near average level in the multiple is a modest disconnect with the financial results and outlook for the company. Furthermore, the Information Technology sector has a median P/S of 2.81 as of this writing and so Dell is at a 75% discount to peers. Even though the fundamentals are mixed, the company remains a key provider of AI servers and will continue to benefit from the infrastructure boom. Therefore, this large of a discount seems unreasonable, further showing that the stock is undervalued at current levels.

Conclusion

In the above analysis, both the technicals and fundamentals are shown to be mixed bags for Dell. The stock is still in a downtrend but there are an abundance of mixed indications in the moving averages and indicators. For the fundamentals, both Q4 results and Q1 guidance were mixed as there is some shakiness in the financials. However, the P/S ratio seems to be a bit depressed currently as it's at an average level relative to past few years. Therefore, there is a modest disconnect with the fundamentals. In addition, the massive valuation gap between Dell and the IT sector also seems excessive. As a result, I reiterate Dell at a buy rating on valuation attractiveness even as technical and fundamental uncertainties persist.

data.title: Smurfit Westrock: Supportive Q1 Delivery, Buy Rating Confirmed
data.description:
We believe the market has yet to fully understand the strategic value of the Smurfit WestRock (NYSE:SW) merger, and we view the current weakness as a strong opportunity to buy the dip. Since our last update, the company's share price declined by an additional 12.92% (Fig. 1), but it released a positive Q1 update and announced facility closure and capacity reduction, favoring its strategy of value over volume. As a reminder, our buy rating was backed by 1) merger upside with synergies and operational efficiencies, 2) potential peace between Russia and Ukraine, 3) its global presence that mitigates risks from tariffs and counter-tariffs, and 4) a discounted valuation vs peers.

Fig 1

Q1 Results and Our Positive Take

Smurfit WestRock reported mixed results for Q1 2025. Net sales, gross profit, and EBIT missed consensus, but net income beat expectations. The company had an EPS of $0.74. The company reported unusually low income tax expense, which is notable considering that sales have grown and this expense is lower than in the previous Q1. While not stated directly in their financial statement, it could be due to WestRock’s tax loss before its merger. Q1 2025 results included top-line sales of $7.65 billion, reflecting the WestRock combination, and an adjusted EBITDA of $1.25 billion, equating to an adjusted EBITDA margin of 16.4%. A DPS of $0.43 has been proposed.

  1. As we know, corrugated packaging volumes are correlated to GDP growth expectations. We believe that, on a short-term basis, packaging companies might be impacted by tariffs and potential retaliation on volume pressure from weaker consumer demand. This was not seen in Q1, but we think the company and other domestic US producers could emerge as key beneficiaries of import substitution in the medium to long run. Additionally, the company sourced raw materials locally except for their Canadian mill. During the Q&A analysts call, we understood that the company is shifting some production from Canada to the USA to avoid tariffs;
  2. The WestRock merger seems to be going well. In Q1, the company internalised synergies of around $80 million. On a positive note, the company's gross synergies of $350 million are expected to be realised in 2025, compared to a previous yearly estimate of $300 million. On a negative note, the company announced an economic downtime of $100 million, which is expected to impact Q2 negatively. However, volumes in North America came in at -4.7% year-on-year, which implies some market share loss, but volumes in APAC and EMEA look solid at +1.5% year-on-year (Fig. 2);
  3. To improve operational efficiency, the company reported the closure of multiple North American and European facilities for a total capacity of 600 kt. The mills closing in the USA are reported to increase the company’s fiscal year 2025 EBITDA by $50 to 60 million and save the company around $100 million in maintenance CAPEX over the next five years (Fig. 3). Furthermore, Smurfit is nearing completion of the biomass boiler they started building in 2022, aligning with their sustainability goals. Important to note that management highlighted some end-market weakness in March and early April, possibly impacting Q2 results. That said, the Q2 adj. EBITDA guidance of $1.2 billion implied a sequential improvement of 4% and no anticipated improvement in trading conditions, highlighting confidence in near-term supportive pricing dynamics. The introduction of Smurfit WestRock's full-year guidance for an adjusted EBITDA of $5.0-5.2 billion positions our forecasts toward the high end of this range but is instructive in helping to frame full-year expectations (Fig. 4). This also reduces our downside risk in valuing the company;
  4. Still related to downside risks, in the Q&A call, we reported the following sentence: "Keep in mind that 75% of our Consumer business is food and beverage. So general in time that this presents slight more resilience, say, the home, health, and beauty pieces." Considering the company's previous track record, this further supports our conviction in the long-term strength of the business.

Source: Smurfit WestRock Q1 results ppt - Fig 2

Fig 3

Fig 4

Last time, we projected a 2025 adjusted EBITDA of $5.5 billion, which is now above the company's yearly guidance. This was supported by higher expectations and tailwinds from US/EU corrugated box pricing increases (€30-80 /ton has been implemented). We believe the company is managing its downside case scenario, and we also believe this integration process will require additional costs for the year. Using the mid-point of the Fiscal Year 2025 guide, the company trades at ~6.9x EV/EBITDA. A comparison of industry peers suggests that Smurfit WestRock appears undervalued, particularly relative to International Paper and Packaging Corporation of America. Smurfit Westrock's stock has been the worst-performing stock YTD in the packaging space, providing an attractive entry point. Applying an FWD sector median multiple of 8x on a lower EBITDA (Fig. 5), our enterprise value reached $40 billion. Our equity value is $27.2 billion, with a target price of $51.48 per share. There is at least a 32% upside to catch. Notably, we see the 2025 EBITDA through the cycle levels, but it still does not reflect the full commercial improvement initiatives.

Fig 5

Risks and Threats

We have extensive coverage of the company's downside risks, so we report the following downside: 1) tariffs uncertainty and Q2 lower sales (due to inventory management from clients), 2) combined deal synergies, 3) integration risks, 4) change in COGS, especially in pulp and energy price, 5) higher competition with an imbalance supply/demand evolution.

Conclusion

Smurfit WestRock presents an attractive value proposition driven by its scale and improving operational efficiency. Given its transitional phase and several moving parts post-merger, the market may not fully reflect its long-term value. For this reason, we continue to buy-rated the company.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 2024 Total Dividend Income And Portfolio Update
data.description:
We all know the routine by now… dividend investing isn’t sexy but, over time, can provide a nice source of passive income and my 2024 totals continue to highlight that fact. My total passive income received in 2024 came out to: $17,595.87. This averages out to well over $1,400 a month in passive income. Of course, my expenses exceed this amount but getting $1,400+ each month definitely helps filling the gas tank or with groceries and more. My total 2023 passive income was $16,011.65, and it was nice to see a year-over-year increase of 9.9% over that time. Again, just highlighting the fact that with continued adding of fresh capital, reinvesting and those occasional dividend increases, passive income can grow with little effort. 2024 also saw some changes to some of my holdings as I shuttled several of my dividend income payers for higher return crypto plays. These will be shorter term holds averaging about two to three years as I plan to sell these assets and pile the cash back into dividend stocks. As you know I have been investing into bitcoin itself since 2017 continually buying and holding that asset on the side. Needless to say, that investment has gone up quite a lot since 2017 and I’m happy to say that I keep buying all the time and have not sold any. My conviction in bitcoin continues to remain strong and that is an asset I’ll gladly hold long into the future.

That being said, my crypto holdings in my brokerage account will be liquidated at some point. Those holdings include several ETFs (Grayscale Bitcoin Trust ETF (GBTC), Grayscale Bitcoin Mini Trust ETF (BTC), Fidelity Wise Origin Bitcoin Fund (FBTC) and iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT), in my ROTH and IRA accounts as well as Strategy Incorporated (MSTR) and T-Rex 2X Long MSTR Daily Target ETF (MSTU) in my regular brokerage account. My current portfolio is now updated to reflect these changes.

I can hear the moans and groans about my sacrilegious actions selling dividend paying stocks for “bitcoin stocks” but the reality is that bitcoin and crypto are here to stay and have the ability to supercharge returns for any portfolio. I have lived through the wild swings since 2017 and by now feel numb to any move up or down that exceeds 50% or more. Over time (since my holding in 2017), bitcoin offered the best returns and still has a lot of runway left. As I mentioned, my “bitcoin stocks” are merely a vehicle to boost my overall brokerage portfolio value with those proceeds ultimately finding a place back into dividend stocks

In the meantime, I continue to buy and hold and reinvest into traditional dividend paying stocks and so far, am looking to exceed my 2024 total passive income this year. I did not lose the plot.

Going forward I plan on looking into adding some income funds to my portfolio as well. I have an eye on three particular names but do now know too much about them. No doubt, my overall portfolio will always be mostly “boring” traditional dividend paying stocks with a sprinkle of some higher yielding income funds.

Hope everyone had a great 2024 and continued heals and success in 2025. I welcome your comments.

Disclosure: Long all above

data.title: 7%+ Yields With Stress-Free Dividend Growth For An Early Retirement
data.description:
Blue-chip stocks that pay 7%+ yields and consistently grow their dividends through thick and thin are indispensable components of a portfolio designed for retiring on dividends. While I recently discussed some of the stocks that belong in this category, such as Altria (MO) and Pfizer (PFE), that I do not think are particularly attractive buys right now, there are some that I like a lot. In this article, I will discuss two of them.

Opportunity #1

The first one is Enterprise Products Partners (EPD). It has the best balance sheet in the midstream sector with an A- credit rating and a leverage ratio of around three times, along with around an 18-year weighted average term to maturity on its debt, with plenty of liquidity and significant free cash flow generation. Additionally, the business generates very stable cash flows through its long-duration contracted pipeline assets and has consistently generated double-digit returns on invested capital even during significant energy sector downturns, such as during the 2020 COVID-19 outbreak and in the 2018 energy sector crash. EPD’s prudent capital allocation has also enabled it to deliver over a quarter-century of consecutive distribution growth. In recent years, it has been growing its distribution at around a 5% CAGR. When you combine that with its 7% current yield, which is covered 1.7 times by distributable cash flow, and its significant growth pipeline that is expected to come online over the course of this year and next year, there is a lot to like here.

In particular, as EPD brings on the significant new EBITDA while also seeing its growth CapEx decline meaningfully over the next few years, it should see significant cash flow capacity due to its leverage ratio declining from its EBITDA increasing, as well as its free cash flow soaring due to the additional EBITDA in combination with its reduced CapEx requirements. Therefore, it should be able to pour a lot of capital into buying back units, growing its distribution, and/or acquiring new assets to fuel further growth. Put all together, EPD makes for one of the most attractive risk-adjusted 7%+ yielding growth investments you can find in the market today. As a result, it is one of our largest positions.

When it comes to risks, there really are not that many with EPD because the management has done such a prudent job of building the business. That said, it does have substantial exposure to energy exports from the U.S., so if current trade negotiations between the Trump administration and major trading partners go south, this could cause some disruption to the business, though it seems more likely than not that the outcome of these trade negotiations will actually lead to increased demand for U.S. energy rather than decreased demand, which means that it will still more likely than not be a tailwind for EPD’s business rather than a headwind. Another thing to keep in mind with EPD is that its unit price in the short term is very sensitive to energy price movements, although in the long term it should have minimal impact given the fact that it has very little actual commodity price sensitivity due to its long-term contracts on its assets.

Opportunity #2

Another highly attractive investment opportunity right now is Hess Midstream (HESM). With HESM, we get a forward 8% yield along with much better dividend growth potential. Additionally, HESM's dividends are tax advantaged (rather than facing foreign withholding tax and potential ADR fees with TEF) and HESM issues a 1099 rather than a K1 like many of its peers do.

HESM also enjoys very stable cash flows, as 85% of its revenues have fixed-fee contracts and 15% have cost-of-service contracts, giving it 100% fee-based contracts with no direct commodity price exposure and inflation escalation, as they also have CPI escalators on them. Additionally, it has minimum volume commitments on 80% of its revenues, which further insulates it from macro-factor-related volatility.

HESM also has a promising growth profile, as it is guided to grow its dividend at a 5% annualized rate through 2027 at least and expects to grow its free cash flow significantly faster than that. Analysts also seem to be buying the dividend growth thesis, as they forecast an 8% CAGR for the dividend through 2029 that combines with the 8% current dividend yield to provide a very attractive yield plus dividend growth story for shareholders moving forward.

As management recently detailed on their latest earnings call, they expect to have $1.25 billion worth of excess capital flexibility through 2027, which will give them enormous flexibility to either buy back a lot more shares and or grow the dividend at an even faster clip than they are now. They can do all this while also achieving their target leverage ratio of 3.0 times, which they are very close to today with a 3.1 times leverage ratio. They also have proven to be very aggressive share repurchasers, as they have repurchased about $100 million a quarter generally, and while they do not have a firm commitment to doing that moving forward, they certainly think that is a very feasible cadence to continue their buybacks moving forward. Management summarized this well on their latest earnings call, stating:

Since the beginning of 2021, we have returned $1.95 billion to shareholders through accretive repurchases. In addition, through the combination of our 5% targeted annual distribution growth and 10 distribution level increases following each repurchase, we have increased our distribution per Class A share by approximately 57% since 2021. As a result, our total shareholder return yield is one of the highest of our midstream peers.

Another reason to like HESM is that they expect to generate about $760 million in free cash flow this year, which is quite attractive relative to their current market cap, as that implies a 17.6% free cash flow yield, which is more than twice their current dividend yield.

The biggest risks surrounding HESM pertain to its pending potential takeover by Chevron (CVX), as Chevron is looking to buy Hess Corporation (HES), which is HESM's major counterparty. However, there is some uncertainty about the timing of the closing of the transaction, since ExxonMobil (XOM) is actually suing based on one of the terms of the deal, and there is expected to be a hearing later this month over it. Based on the outcome of that, there is a chance that the deal may not even go through at all, in which case the status quo would remain in place, which we feel pretty good about. While HES is not the highest quality company, HESM's assets are pretty good quality and have been performing well, and the company continues to have a strong growth profile.

Meanwhile, if CVX does acquire the company successfully, that will greatly improve the credit quality of HESM's main counterparty, though there is also a chance that CVX may try to fully acquire HESM and roll them up under their umbrella. In such a case, though, a worst-case scenario would be them acquiring it probably at a very slight premium to the stock price at the time of the roll-up, but there is also a decent chance that they would have to pay at least a mild premium to the stock price, which could provide further upside as a near-term catalyst. Time will tell, but with HESM, we are buying it with the intent of holding it for the long term for the dividend yield and growth story, but we think that there is potential near-term upside there as well due to a potential roll-up from CVX.

Another factor to keep in mind is that there has been some insider selling recently, along with pretty heavy selling from institutional holders of HESM stock. While this certainly is not a bullish indicator for HESM, I do not think it is nearly the bearish indicator that some may think it is because institutional investors typically sell stakes for a wide variety of reasons, including freeing up capital to allocate to other investments, while HESM's aggressive buybacks indicate that management still believes the stock is a good value as they themselves are shareholders of the company. Furthermore, insider sales this year so far have occurred at prices that are about 10% higher than the price it is trading at today. So this also offsets some of that headwind as well.

Between HESM's substantial distribution coverage ratio, well-diversified assets that have limited commodity price sensitivity, low leverage ratio, and solid long-term growth potential, we think that it makes for a compelling buy right now.

Investor Takeaway

As this article points out, whenever you can find a 7%+ yielding blue-chip investment opportunity that combines a strong balance sheet with an inflation-beating growth profile, it generally makes for an excellent addition to a dividend portfolio for retirement. With EPD and HESM, we think investors have some great opportunities to buy attractive, durable, and growing passive income. As a result, we hold significant positions in both of them in our retirement portfolio at High Yield Investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

The funds are subject to the volatility of the financial markets, including that of equity and fixed income investments in the U.S. and abroad, and may be subject to risks associated with investing in high-yield, small-cap, commodity-linked and foreign securities. Market Update and Portfolio Review

  • Uncertainty about the direction of U.S. policy weighed on markets in Q1, as investors digested news on tariffs, cuts to government programs, and tighter immigration policy. U.S. equities fared poorly, especially large-cap growth and smaller-caps. Tariffs were a catalyst for the repricing of non-U.S equities. Non-U.S. markets' fiscally oriented growth narrative, cheaper valuations, and improved earnings estimates drove one of the largest relative-return differences between U.S. and non-U.S. equities in history. A falling U.S. dollar also boosted non-U.S. stocks. Fixed-income assets served as an equity diversifier amid near-term risks related to economic growth, inflation and the path of interest rates.

Quarterly Performance for Representative Funds

Period Ending March 31, 2025

See the Performance Summary table in this review for performance reporting on all vintages. Funds shown in this chart are representative samples of where a target-date investor might be at various stages of their life.

Performance Attribution Summary

  • Active asset allocation decisions and the performance of the underlying portfolio managers contributed to the fund's performance versus the composite index in Q1. In particular, an overweight in non-U.S. equities and underweight in U.S. equities added meaningful value to the fund's relative result.
  • Among the underlying portfolios, non-U.S. equity portfolio managers, in aggregate, notably contributed to the fund's relative performance, as did U.S. investment-grade bond managers.

Relative Contributors (representative across vintages):

U.S. and non-U.S. equity funds with a value-oriented investment philosophy contributed to the fund's relative result this quarter. The largest fund-level individual contributors:

  • Fidelity® Series Large Cap Stock Fund outperformed its benchmark, the S&P 500® index. The fund emphasizes companies for which long-term normalized earnings and free-cash-flow are underappreciated by investors. Sector allocations notably contributed to the fund's relative outcome, particularly an underweight in information technology. Overweight positions in GE Aerospace (GE) and Exxon Mobil (XOM) were among the fund's top individual relative contributors. Not owning benchmark stock Tesla also helped.
  • Fidelity® Series International Value Fund outpaced its benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Value Index. The fund is managed by Alex Zavratsky, who believes that investing in attractively valued, high-quality companies (e.g., a strong balance sheet, stable return on equity and recurring cash flow) provides the best chance of outperformance in the long run. Outperformance was driven by security selection, primarily in the industrials sector, where an out-of-benchmark position in German auto and arms manufacturer Rheinmetall (OTCPK:RNMBF) was the largest individual relative contributor. A non-benchmark stake in British-based defense and aerospace firm BAE Systems (OTCPK:BAESF) also helped, along with an overweight in Banco Santander (SAN).
  • Fidelity® Series Emerging Market Opportunities Fund outperformed its benchmark, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index. The fund is managed by a team of sector-based portfolio managers who specialize in security selection within their respective sectors. Security selection within the communication services, materials and health care sectors lifted the fund's relative result. Top individual relative contributors included an out-of-benchmark position in Singapore-based digital entertainment, e-commerce and digital finance conglomerate Sea, and overweight stakes in South African firm Impala Platinum Holdings (OTCQX:IMPUY) and multinational commerce group PDD Holdings (PDD).

Relative Detractors (representative across vintages):

U.S. and non-U.S. equity portfolios with a growth bias generally detracted this quarter. The largest fund-level individual detractors:

  • Fidelity® Series Growth Company Fund underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 3000® Growth Index. The fund is managed by Steve Wymer, who invests across a spectrum of companies, from blue chip growth to aggressive growth. Steve focuses on firms operating in well-positioned industries and niches, and those he considers capable of delivering persistent sales and earnings growth. Security selection within the health care sector notably detracted from relative performance for the quarter. The largest individual relative detractors included a larger-than-index stake in footwear designer and distributor Deckers Outdoor (DECK), along with semiconductor companies Nvidia (NVDA) and Astera Labs (ALAB).
  • Fidelity® Series Blue Chip Growth Fund trailed its benchmark, the Russell 1000® Growth Index. The fund is managed by Sonu Kalra, who invests in companies that he believes have above-average earnings-growth potential and a sustainable business model. Security selection within information technology, including overweights in Marvell Technology (MRVL), Astera Labs and Nvidia, meaningfully detracted from performance. An underweight and security selection in the financials sector also hurt.

Active Asset Allocation:

Active asset allocation positioning had a positive influence on the fund's relative performance this quarter, especially an overweight in non-U.S. equities and an underweight in U.S. equities.

Outlook and Positioning

  • The fund's glide path and strategic asset allocation reflect our long-term views and insights on participant needs, diversification and capital markets. Our investment process focuses on selecting strategic asset classes that provide appealing long-term returns, independent sources of return and risk, and favorable portfolio implementation attributes.
  • We maintain the view that trends in the economy and policy conditions may imply a regime change for financial markets. High debt, aging demographics, peak globalization, and geopolitics continue to create uncertainty in the path of inflation, policy and corporate profits.
  • With this in mind, strategic asset allocation decisions continue to emphasize diversification to help us navigate different risks. In recent years, we have amplified portfolio diversification based on a view that participants will experience multiple market environments throughout their lifetime, and that regimes can change abruptly.
  • Over long periods, equities have been a powerful asset for building wealth, while our fixed-income portfolio provides balance during periods of inflationary and deflationary stress. The fund's active allocation positioning reflects intermediate-term views on assets that we believe are mispriced relative to our view of fair value.
  • We expect the U.S. economy to muddle through tariff-induced challenges because of its large, diversified, services-based economy, with resilient companies and consumer-related sectors. We also acknowledge the considerable uncertainty about the outlook for the domestic economy, as investors, consumers and businesses assess the likely duration and impact of policy changes.
  • While non-U.S. companies are facing the risk of weaker demand from U.S. consumers, a number of factors may buffer the impact, including lower starting valuations and earnings expectations, fiscal room for governments to offset the impact of tariffs, and the opportunity to repatriate capital that has flowed into U.S. assets over the past decade.
  • We believe the range of possible outcomes is wide and the landscape is changing rapidly. This has caused volatility in both equity and fixed-income markets. We continue to assess incoming information as it occurs, in collaboration with analysts across Fidelity's multi-asset, equity and fixed-income teams. We continue to evaluate the fund's positioning, while adhering to our diversified, long-term approach.
  • In our view, heightened volatility and the persistence of uncertainty in the capital markets reinforce the importance of a disciplined investment process. Our target-date strategies are managed with a long-term investment horizon and provide investors with a diversified portfolio that seeks to balance their need for total returns with their sensitivity to many risks – longevity, market, inflation and deflation. During the 29 years we have managed target-date funds, this approach has proven to be a prudent and time-tested strategy. Since inception of Fidelity's target-date strategies, we believe we have achieved successful outcomes, as measured by total returns and by investors' tendency to remain invested throughout periods of volatility. We remain focused on researching the key drivers of outcomes for retirement plan participants and retirement investors, including capital markets, diversification and future expectations of participant attributes.

Dow Jones U.S. Total Stock Market Index is a float-adjusted market-capitalization-weighted index of all equity securities of U.S. headquartered companies with readily available price data.

MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (Net MA Tax) is a market- capitalization-weighted index designed to measure the investable equity market performance for global investors of large and mid-cap stocks in developed and emerging markets, excluding the United States. Index returns are adjusted for tax withholding rates applicable to U.S. based mutual funds organized as Massachusetts business trusts.

Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS) 0-5 Year Index (Series-L) is a market value-weighted index that measures the performance of inflation-protected securities issued by the US Treasury that have a remaining average life of less than 5 years.

Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS) 5+ Year Index (Series-L) is a market value-weighted index that measures the performance of inflation-protected securities issued by the US Treasury that have a remaining average life of 5 or more.

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based, market- value-weighted benchmark that measures the performance of the investment grade, U.S. dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market. Sectors in the index include Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, MBS (agency fixed-rate and hybrid ARM pass-throughs), ABS, and CMBS.

Bloomberg U.S. Long Treasury is a market value-weighted index of investment-grade fixed-rate public obligations of the U.S. Treasury with maturities of ten years or more.

Bloomberg Global Aggregate Treasury Ex USD Ex EM RIC Capped Float Adjusted Index is a customized subset of the Global Aggregate Treasury Index that meets the same diversification guidelines that a fund must pass to qualify as a regulated investment company (RIC). This multi-currency benchmark includes fixed-rate treasury bonds from developed markets issuers while excluding USD denominated debt. The index also adjusts the par amount outstanding of GBP-, JPY-, and CAD-denominated bonds for Central Bank purchases in each of the respective currencies.

Bloomberg U.S. 3-6 Month Treasury Bills Index is a market- capitalization-weighted index of investment-grade, fixed-rate public obligations of the U.S. Treasury with remaining maturities from 3 up to (but not including) 6 months, excluding zero coupon strips.

IMPORTANT FUND INFORMATION

Relative positioning data presented in this commentary is based on the fund's primary benchmark (index) unless a secondary benchmark is provided to assess performance.

There are multiple drivers of performance for the Funds. Strategic asset allocation is expected to be the primary source of returns over long-term periods, while incremental excess return can result from active asset allocation and building-block manager decisions. Strategic allocation decisions are based on research and insights related to the factors that influence outcomes over long-term periods, including diversification, capital markets and participant behaviors. We periodically increase the diversification of our strategic asset allocation in an attempt to better navigate capital market uncertainty. Active asset allocation is intended to provide shareholders with exposure to asset classes that are mispriced and may offer additional returns and opportunities for risk mitigation. Active allocation expands the investment opportunity set and provides flexibility to adjust asset class exposures over intermediate-term periods. Allocations to active building-block managers offer shareholders the opportunity to benefit from informational advantages across Fidelity's asset management platform.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. R- Squared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.
